message.content:
Mechanical AsciiDoc edit task. You are editing 4 detail-site subdoc files to insert an axhopt_typed_parameter marker.

For EACH of the 4 files listed below:

1. Read the file with the Read tool.
2. Find the FIRST `//axhoq_precondition` line in the file.
3. Use the Edit tool to insert the following two lines immediately before that `//axhoq_precondition` line, with a blank line separating the new block from the existing precondition:

```
//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burn_alias}
```

The result should look like:
```
[end of NOTE prose paragraph]

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burn_alias}

//axhoq_precondition
[existing precondition text...]
```

To make the Edit's old_string unique, include the entire first precondition (the `//axhoq_precondition` line plus all content lines up through but not including the next `//axho` marker).

Do NOT modify any other content. Do NOT add any other markers or content. Each file gets exactly ONE insertion.

Files (absolute paths):
1. /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSTLR-NodeRegimeRender.adoc
2. /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSTLV-NodeRegimeValidate.adoc
3. /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSTGC-GarrisonCygwin.adoc
4. /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSTGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc

Report back with the count of files successfully edited.

content.text: I'll read each file, locate the first `//axhoq_precondition` line, and insert the marker block before it.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_node_regime_render}
3	
4	NOTE: Prints the resolved field set for a single {burn_regime} profile
5	identified by a positional {burn_alias} argument.
6	Read-only; no remote contact.
7	
8	//axhoq_precondition
9	A {burn_regime} profile with the given {burn_alias} exists in the local
10	station subtree under the current {burs_user}.
11	
12	//axhos_step
13	. *Locate profile*: resolve the profile file for the supplied
14	{burn_alias} under the station subtree
15	
16	//axhos_step
17	. *Resolve fields*: parse the profile and emit every defined
18	{burn_regime} field assignment
19	
20	//axhog_guarantee
21	No remote network contact occurred and no field was modified on disk.
22	
23	//axhoc_completion
24	Exit 0 on successful render.
25	Non-zero when the {burn_alias} is not found or the profile fails to
26	parse.
27	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_node_regime_validate}
3	
4	NOTE: Validates a single {burn_regime} profile, identified by positional
5	{burn_alias}, for required-field presence, valid {burn_tier}, and
6	key-file accessibility.
7	Read-only; no remote contact.
8	
9	//axhoq_precondition
10	A {burn_regime} profile with the given {burn_alias} exists in the local
11	station subtree.
12	
13	//axhos_step
14	. *Load profile*: parse the profile file for the supplied {burn_alias}
15	
16	//axhos_step
17	. *Required fields*: confirm {burn_host}, {burn_user}, {burn_alias},
18	and {burn_tier} are present
19	
20	//axhos_step
21	. *Tier sanity*: confirm {burn_tier} is exactly one of
22	`privileged | workload`
23	
24	//axhos_step
25	. *Key reachable*: resolve {burn_key_file} and confirm the file is
26	readable by the calling user
27	
28	//axhog_guarantee
29	The verdict reflects the on-disk state at call time; no field was
30	modified.
31	
32	//axhoc_completion
33	Exit 0 when every check passes.
34	Non-zero with a diagnostic message identifying the first failed check.
35	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_cygwin}
3	
4	NOTE: Establishes privileged BURN trust against a Cygwin node by writing
5	the privileged {burn_regime} profile and driving the Cygwin-specific
6	{busn_garrison} ceremony.
7	Idempotent on re-run.
8	
9	//axhoq_precondition
10	The remote Windows account named in {burn_user} is a member of the
11	local Administrators group on the target node.
12	
13	//axhoq_precondition
14	OpenSSH server is reachable on {burn_host} from the calling station,
15	and the operator can complete the console-side handshake at the
16	Windows keyboard (or RDP).
17	
18	//axhos_step
19	. *Write profile*: persist the privileged {burn_regime} profile to the
20	station subtree with `{burn_tier} = privileged`
21	
22	//axhos_step
23	. *Console handshake*: drive the Cygwin-specific console ceremony to
24	install the station pubkey into
25	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`
26	
27	//axhos_step
28	. *Probe trust*: confirm passwordless SSH to the privileged account
29	succeeds via {bust_knock}
30	
31	//axhog_guarantee
32	The privileged {burn_regime} profile is persisted locally and the
33	station's {burn_ssh_pubkey} is trusted on the remote Cygwin node.
34	
35	//axhoc_completion
36	Exit 0 when the trust handshake completes and the verification probe
37	succeeds.
38	Non-zero on profile-write failure, console-handshake refusal, or final
39	SSH probe failure.
40	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: Establishes privileged BURN trust against a WSL node by writing
5	the privileged {burn_regime} profile and driving the WSL-specific
6	{busn_garrison} ceremony.
7	Idempotent on re-run.
8	
9	//axhoq_precondition
10	The remote Windows account hosting the WSL distribution is a member of
11	the local Administrators group, and the WSL distribution named in
12	{burn_command} is registered for that account on the target node.
13	
14	//axhoq_precondition
15	OpenSSH server is reachable on {burn_host} from the calling station,
16	and the operator can complete the console-side handshake at the
17	Windows keyboard (or RDP).
18	
19	//axhos_step
20	. *Write profile*: persist the privileged {burn_regime} profile to the
21	station subtree with `{burn_tier} = privileged` and the WSL launcher
22	recorded in {burn_command}
23	
24	//axhos_step
25	. *Console handshake*: drive the WSL-specific console ceremony — bash
26	owns control flow and PowerShell is the syscall layer — to install the
27	station pubkey into
28	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`
29	
30	//axhos_step
31	. *Probe trust*: confirm passwordless SSH to the privileged account
32	succeeds via {bust_knock}
33	
34	//axhog_guarantee
35	The privileged {burn_regime} profile is persisted locally and the
36	station's {burn_ssh_pubkey} is trusted on the remote WSL node.
37	
38	//axhoc_completion
39	Exit 0 when the trust handshake completes and the verification probe
40	succeeds.
41	Non-zero on profile-write failure, WSL launcher unavailability,
42	console-handshake refusal, or final SSH probe failure.
43	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>
